courses.title: Terminal Mastery
courses.description: Master the command line from basics to advanced workflows. Navigate filesystems, manipulate files, chain commands, and automate tasks like a pro.
courses.shortDescription: From zero to terminal hero — navigation, file ops, power tools, scripting, and remote management.

courses.title: Git & GitHub Professional
courses.description: Professional version control workflows. Learn branching strategies, rebasing, advanced history manipulation, and CI/CD with GitHub Actions.
courses.shortDescription: Commits, branching, rebasing, cherry-pick, bisect, submodules, and GitHub Actions.

courses.title: Backend Servers with Hono
courses.description: Build blazing-fast backend APIs with Hono. Routing, middleware, validation, CORS, error handling, rate limiting, and file uploads.
courses.shortDescription: Routing, middleware, Zod validation, CORS, error handling, rate limiting, file uploads.

courses.title: Database Mastery with Drizzle
courses.description: Type-safe database operations with Drizzle ORM. Schema design, CRUD, relations, migrations, transactions, indexing, and query optimization.
courses.shortDescription: Schema design, CRUD, relations, migrations, transactions, indexing, query optimization.

courses.title: Deployment & DevOps
courses.description: Deploy production-grade applications. VPS setup with Ubuntu, Nginx + SSL, PM2 process management, Docker basics, and CI/CD pipelines.
courses.shortDescription: VPS, Nginx + SSL, PM2, Docker basics, CI/CD with GitHub Actions.

courses.title: Authentication Mastery
courses.description: Secure user authentication systems. Google + Kimi OAuth flows, JWT sessions, protected routes, refresh tokens, and role-based access control.
courses.shortDescription: Google + Kimi OAuth, JWT, protected routes, refresh tokens, role-based guards.

knowledgebase.title: Navigation Commands
knowledgebase.content:
Directory Navigation

Move through your filesystem with precision.

$ pwd                          # Print working directory
$ cd ~                         # Go to home directory
$ cd -                         # Go to previous directory
$ cd ..                        # Go up one directory
$ cd ../..                     # Go up two directories
$ pushd /var/log               # Push directory onto stack
$ popd                         # Pop directory from stack
$ dirs -v                      # View directory stack

Listing Files

$ ls                           # List files
$ ls -la                       # List all with details
$ ls -lah                      # Human-readable sizes
$ ls -ltr                      # Sort by time, reversed
$ ls -R                        # Recursive list
$ tree                         # Directory tree view
$ tree -L 2                    # Limit depth to 2 levels

Path Shortcuts

  • ~ — Home directory
  • . — Current directory
  • .. — Parent directory
  • - — Previous directory (cd -)
  • Tab — Auto-complete paths

knowledgebase.title: File & Directory Operations
knowledgebase.content:
Create & Remove

$ mkdir newdir                 # Create directory
$ mkdir -p a/b/c               # Create nested directories
$ touch file.txt               # Create empty file
$ touch file{1,2,3}.txt        # Create multiple files
$ rm file.txt                  # Remove file
$ rm -rf dir/                  # Force remove directory
$ rmdir emptydir               # Remove empty directory

Copy & Move

$ cp file.txt backup.txt       # Copy file
$ cp -r src/ dest/             # Copy directory recursively
$ cp -a src/ dest/             # Preserve attributes
$ mv old.txt new.txt           # Rename file
$ mv file.txt dir/             # Move file
$ mv *.txt archive/            # Move all matching files

Links & Permissions

$ ln -s target link            # Symbolic link
$ ln target hardlink           # Hard link
$ chmod 755 script.sh          # rwxr-xr-x
$ chmod u+x script.sh          # Add execute for owner
$ chown user:group file.txt    # Change owner
$ stat file.txt                # File metadata

Flags Quick Reference

  • -r — Recursive (directories)
  • -f — Force (no prompts)
  • -i — Interactive (confirm)
  • -v — Verbose (show actions)
  • -p — Preserve / parents

knowledgebase.title: Git Commands
knowledgebase.content:
Basic Workflow

$ git status                   # Check repository state
$ git add file.txt             # Stage specific file
$ git add .                    # Stage all changes
$ git add -p                   # Interactive staging
$ git commit -m "message"      # Commit with message
$ git commit -am "message"   # Add and commit tracked files
$ git log --oneline            # Compact history
$ git log --graph --decorate   # Visual branch graph
$ git diff                     # Unstaged changes
$ git diff --staged            # Staged changes

Branching

$ git branch feature-x         # Create branch
$ git checkout feature-x       # Switch branch
$ git checkout -b feature-x    # Create and switch
$ git merge feature-x          # Merge into current
$ git branch -d feature-x      # Delete merged branch
$ git branch -D feature-x      # Force delete branch

Remote Operations

$ git remote -v                # List remotes
$ git push -u origin main      # Push and set upstream
$ git pull origin main         # Fetch and merge
$ git fetch origin             # Download refs only
$ git push origin --delete branch  # Delete remote branch

Undo Operations

$ git reset HEAD file.txt      # Unstage file
$ git checkout -- file.txt     # Discard changes
$ git revert HEAD              # Create undo commit
$ git reset --soft HEAD~1      # Undo last commit (keep staged)
$ git reset --hard HEAD~1      # Destroy last commit

knowledgebase.title: Docker Commands
knowledgebase.content:
Containers

$ docker ps                    # List running containers
$ docker ps -a                 # List all containers
$ docker run -it ubuntu bash   # Interactive container
$ docker run -d -p 3000:3000 myapp  # Detached with port
$ docker stop container_id     # Stop container
$ docker rm container_id       # Remove container
$ docker rm -f $(docker ps -aq)  # Remove all containers
$ docker logs -f container_id  # Follow logs

Images

$ docker images                # List images
$ docker build -t myapp .      # Build image
$ docker pull node:20          # Pull image
$ docker rmi image_id          # Remove image
$ docker system prune          # Clean unused data

Compose

$ docker-compose up            # Start services
$ docker-compose up -d         # Start detached
$ docker-compose down          # Stop and remove
$ docker-compose ps            # List services
$ docker-compose logs -f       # Follow logs
$ docker-compose build         # Rebuild services

Inspect

$ docker inspect container_id  # Container details
$ docker exec -it container_id sh  # Enter running container
$ docker top container_id      # Running processes
$ docker stats                 # Resource usage

knowledgebase.title: Power Tools — grep, find, sed, awk
knowledgebase.content:
grep — Pattern Search

$ grep "error" log.txt         # Basic search
$ grep -i "error" log.txt    # Case-insensitive
$ grep -r "TODO" src/        # Recursive
$ grep -E "error|warn" log.txt  # Extended regex
$ grep -v "debug" log.txt    # Invert match
$ grep -n "pattern" file.txt  # Show line numbers
$ grep -c "pattern" file.txt  # Count matches

find — File Search

$ find . -name "*.js"        # By name
$ find . -type f -mtime -1     # Modified in last day
$ find . -type f -size +1M     # Larger than 1MB
$ find . -name "*.log" -delete  # Find and delete
$ find . -type f | xargs grep "pattern"  # Combine with grep

sed — Stream Editor

$ sed 's/foo/bar/g' file.txt   # Replace all
$ sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt  # Edit in place
$ sed -n '10,20p' file.txt     # Print lines 10-20
$ sed '/pattern/d' file.txt    # Delete matching lines
$ sed '1d' file.txt            # Delete first line

awk — Text Processing

$ awk '{print $1}' file.txt     # First column
$ awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd  # Custom delimiter
$ awk 'NR>1 {sum+=$3} END {print sum}' data.csv  # Sum column
$ awk '/pattern/ {print $0}' file.txt  # Filter and print

Combination Patterns

$ find . -name "*.js" | xargs rm              # Delete all JS files
$ cat data.json | jq '.users[] | .email'       # Extract emails
$ ps aux | grep node | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill  # Kill node processes

knowledgebase.title: Nginx Configuration
knowledgebase.content:
Basic Server Block

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name example.com;
    root /var/www/html;
    index index.html;

    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ =404;
    }
}

Reverse Proxy

location / {
    proxy_pass http://localhost:3000;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection 'upgrade';
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_cache_bypass $http_upgrade;
}

SSL with Certbot

$ sudo certbot --nginx -d example.com
$ sudo certbot renew --dry-run  # Test renewal
$ sudo systemctl restart nginx   # Restart

Common Directives

  • proxy_pass — Forward to backend
  • try_files — Attempt file resolution
  • rewrite — URL rewriting
  • gzip — Compression

knowledgebase.title: SSH & Remote Management
knowledgebase.content:
Connection

$ ssh user@server.com          # Basic SSH
$ ssh -i key.pem user@host     # With private key
$ ssh -p 2222 user@host        # Custom port
$ ssh -A user@host             # Forward agent

Key Management

$ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "email"  # Generate key
$ ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096    # RSA key
$ ssh-copy-id user@host        # Copy public key
$ ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_rsa        # Add to agent
$ ssh-add -l                   # List loaded keys

File Transfer

$ scp file.txt user@host:/path  # Copy to remote
$ scp -r local/ user@host:remote/  # Copy directory
$ rsync -avz local/ user@host:remote/  # Sync
$ rsync -avz --delete local/ user@host:remote/  # Exact mirror

Config File (~/.ssh/config)

Host myserver
    HostName 192.168.1.100
    User admin
    Port 2222
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_rsa
    ServerAliveInterval 60

knowledgebase.title: tmux & Terminal Multiplexing
knowledgebase.content:
Sessions

$ tmux new -s mysession        # New named session
$ tmux ls                      # List sessions
$ tmux attach -t mysession     # Attach to session
$ tmux kill-session -t mysession  # Kill session
$ tmux rename-session -t old new

Key Bindings (Prefix + Key)

Default prefix: Ctrl+B

Prefix + %    — Split vertically
Prefix + "    — Split horizontally
Prefix + c    — New window
Prefix + n    — Next window
Prefix + p    — Previous window
Prefix + d    — Detach
Prefix + x    — Kill pane
Prefix + z    — Zoom pane
Prefix + ,    — Rename window
Prefix + [    — Scroll mode (q to exit)

Configuration (~/.tmux.conf)

set -g mouse on
set -g prefix C-a
unbind C-b
bind C-a send-prefix
set -g status-style bg=black,fg=white
setw -g mode-keys vi

knowledgebase.title: Permission Denied
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

$ ./script.sh
bash: ./script.sh: Permission denied

Cause

The file lacks execute permission.

Fix

$ chmod +x script.sh           # Add execute permission
$ ls -l script.sh              # Verify permissions

Alternative

$ bash script.sh               # Run with interpreter (no exec bit needed)
$ sh script.sh                 # Same with sh

Common Permission Scenarios

$ chmod 755 script.sh          # rwxr-xr-x (owner full, group/other read+exec)
$ chmod 644 file.txt           # rw-r--r-- (owner read+write, others read)
$ chmod 600 secret.key         # rw------- (owner only)
$ sudo chown user:group file   # Change ownership

knowledgebase.title: Port Already in Use
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

Error: listen EADDRINUSE: address already in use :::3000

Find the Process

$ lsof -ti:3000              # Get PID
$ netstat -tlnp | grep 3000  # Show listener
$ ss -tlnp | grep 3000       # Modern alternative

Kill the Process

$ kill -9 $(lsof -ti:3000)   # Force kill
$ fuser -k 3000/tcp          # Kill by port

Alternative: Use Different Port

$ PORT=3001 npm start        # Override port
$ node server.js --port 3001   # Pass flag

Prevention

Use a process manager like PM2 to avoid orphaned processes:

$ pm2 start app.js            # Managed process
$ pm2 list                    # View running
$ pm2 kill                    # Clean shutdown

knowledgebase.title: SSH Key Authentication Failing
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

$ ssh user@server
Permission denied (publickey,gssapi-keyex,gssapi-with-mic)

Checklist

  1. Correct permissions on local key files
  2. Key loaded in ssh-agent
  3. Public key present on server
  4. Server allows key authentication

Local Fixes

$ chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa      # Private key must be 600
$ chmod 700 ~/.ssh             # Directory must be 700
$ ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_rsa        # Add to agent
$ ssh-add -l                   # Verify loaded

Server Verification

# On the remote server:
$ cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys   # Verify your key is present
$ sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
# Ensure these lines exist:
# PubkeyAuthentication yes
# PasswordAuthentication no
$ sudo systemctl restart sshd

Debug Mode

$ ssh -vvv user@server         # Verbose output for diagnosis

knowledgebase.title: Database Connection Failed
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

Error: connect ECONNREFUSED
Error: Can't reach database server

Checklist

  1. Verify DATABASE_URL — Check host, port, username, password
  2. Check if DB server is running
  3. Firewall rules — Port 3306 (MySQL) or 4000 (TiDB) must be open
  4. SSL configuration — Cloud databases require SSL

Diagnostics

$ mysql -u user -p -h host -P 4000  # Test with CLI
$ telnet host 3306                  # Test port connectivity
$ ping host                         # Test network reachability

SSL Fix for Cloud DBs

# Correct DATABASE_URL format:
DATABASE_URL=mysql://user:pass@host:4000/db?ssl={"rejectUnauthorized":true}

# Wrong:
DATABASE_URL=mysql://user:pass@host:4000/db?ssl=true

Drizzle Connection

import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql2'
const db = drizzle(connection, {
  mode: 'planetscale',  // Required for TiDB
  schema
})

knowledgebase.title: Git Merge Conflicts
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

$ git merge feature-branch
Auto-merging src/App.tsx
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/App.tsx
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and commit the result.

Identify Conflicts

$ git status                    # Shows conflicted files
$ grep -r "<<<<<<<" .          # Find all conflict markers

Conflict Markers Explained

<<<<<<< HEAD
// Your current branch's version
=======
// The branch you're merging's version
>>>>>>> feature-branch

Resolution Steps

  1. Open conflicted files in your editor
  2. Search for <<<<<<< markers
  3. Choose which version to keep (or combine both)
  4. Delete the markers
  5. Stage and commit
$ git add src/App.tsx          # Stage resolved file
$ git commit -m "Resolve merge conflicts"  # Complete merge

Abort Merge

$ git merge --abort             # Cancel merge, restore pre-merge state

knowledgebase.title: npm / Node Version Issues
knowledgebase.content:
Symptom

npm ERR! Unsupported engine
npm ERR! node version mismatch
# or
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'

Check Node Version

$ node --version               # Check current
$ npm --version                # Check npm
$ nvm list                     # List installed versions
$ nvm use 20                   # Switch to Node 20
$ nvm alias default 20         # Set default

Clean Install

$ rm -rf node_modules          # Remove modules
$ rm package-lock.json         # Remove lock
$ npm cache clean --force      # Clear cache
$ npm install                  # Fresh install

Package Scripts

{
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=20.0.0"
  }
}

knowledgebase.title: Shell
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

A shell is a command-line interpreter that takes commands from the keyboard and translates them into instructions for the computer. It sits between you and the operating system kernel.

Common Shells

  • Bash — Bourne Again Shell, the default on most Linux systems
  • Zsh — Z Shell, popular with Oh My Zsh framework
  • Fish — Friendly Interactive Shell, with auto-suggestions
  • sh — Original Bourne Shell, POSIX-compliant

Shell Scripting

#!/bin/bash                     # Shebang line
set -euo pipefail               # Strict mode

# Variables
NAME="World"
echo "Hello, $NAME!"

knowledgebase.title: Repository
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

A repository (repo) is a storage location for code and its complete history. Git repositories contain all files, branches, tags, and every commit ever made.

Repository Types

  • Local repository — On your machine, the .git directory
  • Remote repository — On a server (GitHub, GitLab, etc.)
  • Bare repository — No working directory, only git data

Key Concepts

$ git init                      # Create new repository
$ git clone url                 # Copy existing repository
$ git remote -v                 # View remote URLs
$ git log --oneline             # View commit history

knowledgebase.title: Middleware
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

Middleware is software that sits between an operating system and applications, or between different applications. In web development, middleware functions process requests before they reach route handlers.

Web Middleware Example (Hono)

import { Hono } from 'hono'

const app = new Hono()

// Logging middleware
app.use('*', async (c, next) => {
  console.log(`${c.req.method} ${c.req.url}`)
  await next()
})

// Auth middleware
app.use('/admin/*', async (c, next) => {
  if (!c.req.header('Authorization')) {
    return c.text('Unauthorized', 401)
  }
  await next()
})

Common Middleware Types

  • Authentication — Verify user identity
  • Logging — Record requests
  • CORS — Cross-origin resource sharing
  • Rate limiting — Prevent abuse
  • Error handling — Catch and format errors

knowledgebase.title: ORM (Object-Relational Mapping)
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

An ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) is a technique that lets you query and manipulate data from a database using an object-oriented paradigm instead of writing raw SQL.

Drizzle ORM Example

import { mysqlTable, serial, varchar } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql-core'

// Define schema
export const users = mysqlTable('users', {
  id: serial('id').primaryKey(),
  name: varchar('name', { length: 255 }),
  email: varchar('email', { length: 320 }).unique()
})

// Query
const allUsers = await db.select().from(users)
const user = await db.query.users.findFirst({
  where: eq(users.id, 1)
})

Benefits

  • Type safety — TypeScript knows your tables
  • No SQL injection — Parameterized queries by default
  • Migrations — Schema versioning built-in
  • Relations — Define one-to-many, many-to-many

knowledgebase.title: JWT (JSON Web Token)
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. Used for stateless authentication.

Structure

header.payload.signature

// Header: Algorithm & type
{ "alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT" }

// Payload: Claims
{ "sub": "123", "name": "Dev", "iat": 1704067200 }

// Signature: HMAC-SHA256
HMACSHA256(base64(header) + "." + base64(payload), secret)

Usage

import { sign, verify } from 'jsonwebtoken'

const token = sign({ userId: 1 }, 'secret', { expiresIn: '7d' })
const payload = verify(token, 'secret')  // { userId: 1, iat: ..., exp: ... }

Best Practices

  • Store in httpOnly cookies to prevent XSS
  • Use short expiry with refresh tokens
  • Keep payload minimal — no sensitive data

knowledgebase.title: Reverse Proxy
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

A reverse proxy is a server that sits between client devices and backend servers, forwarding client requests to the appropriate backend server. The client only ever talks to the proxy.

Why Use One?

  • SSL termination — Handles HTTPS at the edge
  • Load balancing — Distributes traffic across servers
  • Caching — Serves static content faster
  • Security — Hides backend server details

Nginx Example

server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name api.example.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://backend:3000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    }
}

Popular Options

  • Nginx — Most popular, lightweight
  • Traefik — Cloud-native, Docker-aware
  • Caddy — Automatic HTTPS

knowledgebase.title: Container
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

A container is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes everything needed to run an application: code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings.

Container vs VM

Virtual Machine:     Container:
+ Guest OS            + Shared Host OS
+ Heavy (GBs)          + Light (MBs)
+ Slow boot            + Instant start
+ Full isolation       + Process isolation

Docker Basics

$ docker build -t myapp .      # Build image
$ docker run -p 3000:3000 myapp  # Run container
$ docker ps                    # List running
$ docker-compose up            # Multi-container

Dockerfile Example

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

knowledgebase.title: CI/CD
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) automates the process of building, testing, and deploying code changes to production.

Pipeline Stages

  1. Code — Developer pushes to repository
  2. Build — Compile, bundle, create artifacts
  3. Test — Run unit, integration, E2E tests
  4. Deploy — Push to staging / production

GitHub Actions Example

name: Deploy
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
  deploy:
    needs: test
    steps:
      - run: echo "Deploying..."

Popular Tools

  • GitHub Actions — Native to GitHub
  • GitLab CI — Built into GitLab
  • Jenkins — Self-hosted, highly configurable

knowledgebase.title: OAuth 2.0
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that enables third-party applications to obtain limited access to a user's resources without exposing their password.

Authorization Code Flow

  1. User clicks "Sign in with Google"
  2. App redirects to Google's authorization server
  3. User authenticates and approves scopes
  4. Google redirects back with authorization code
  5. App exchanges code for access token
  6. App uses token to fetch user profile

Token Types

Access Token    — Short-lived (15 min), used for API calls
Refresh Token   — Long-lived (7 days), gets new access tokens
ID Token        — JWT with user identity claims

Security Considerations

  • Always use PKCE for mobile/SPA apps
  • Use state parameter to prevent CSRF
  • Store tokens securely (httpOnly cookies)

knowledgebase.title: Cron
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

Cron is a time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems. Users schedule scripts or commands to run at fixed times, dates, or intervals using a crontab file.

Crontab Format

┌───────────── minute (0 - 59)
│ ┌───────────── hour (0 - 23)
│ │ ┌───────────── day of month (1 - 31)
│ │ │ ┌───────────── month (1 - 12)
│ │ │ │ ┌───────────── day of week (0 - 6)
│ │ │ │ │
* * * * * command

Common Patterns

0 2 * * * /backup.sh          # Daily at 2 AM
*/15 * * * * /check.sh        # Every 15 minutes
0 0 * * 0 /weekly.sh          # Weekly on Sunday
0 0 1 * * /monthly.sh         # Monthly on 1st
@reboot /startup.sh            # On system boot

Commands

$ crontab -e                   # Edit crontab
$ crontab -l                   # List jobs
$ crontab -r                   # Remove all jobs

knowledgebase.title: tRPC
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

tRPC is a TypeScript RPC framework that enables end-to-end type-safe APIs without code generation. Your frontend and backend share TypeScript types automatically.

Why tRPC?

  • Type safety — Frontend knows exact API shape
  • No codegen — Types flow directly from server
  • Lightweight — Thin wrapper over HTTP
  • Framework agnostic — Works with React, Vue, Svelte

Basic Router

import { initTRPC } from '@trpc/server'

const t = initTRPC.create()

export const appRouter = t.router({
  greeting: t.procedure
    .input(z.string())
    .query(({ input }) => `Hello ${input}!`)
})

// Frontend (fully typed!)
const result = trpc.greeting.useQuery('World')

Integration

tRPC works great with Hono for the backend and React Query on the frontend for caching and mutations.

knowledgebase.title: Drizzle ORM
knowledgebase.content:
Definition

Drizzle ORM is a type-safe SQL-like ORM for TypeScript. It lets you write database queries that look and feel like SQL while maintaining full TypeScript type safety.

Philosophy

Drizzle is SQL-like, not query-builder-like. If you know SQL, you already know Drizzle.

Schema Definition

import { mysqlTable, serial, varchar, timestamp } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql-core'

export const users = mysqlTable('users', {
  id: serial('id').primaryKey(),
  name: varchar('name', { length: 255 }).notNull(),
  email: varchar('email', { length: 320 }).notNull().unique(),
  createdAt: timestamp('createdAt').defaultNow().notNull()
})

Querying

// Select
const users = await db.select().from(users)

// With filter
const user = await db.query.users.findFirst({
  where: eq(users.id, 1)
})

// Relations
const usersWithPosts = await db.query.users.findMany({
  with: { posts: true }
})

Migrations

$ npm run db:push              # Sync schema (dev)
$ npm run db:generate          # Generate migration
$ npm run db:migrate           # Apply migrations

lessons.title: Terminal Basics & Navigation
lessons.content:
Terminal Basics & Navigation

The terminal is a text-based interface to your computer. Unlike a GUI where you point and click, the terminal lets you issue precise commands.

Key Commands

pwd          # Print working directory
cd ~         # Go to home directory
cd ..        # Go up one directory
ls -la       # List all files with details
ls -lah      # Human-readable sizes
clear        # Clear the screen

Navigation Tips

  • Use Tab for auto-completion
  • Use Ctrl+R to search command history
  • ~ is a shortcut to your home directory

quiz.question: What does 'cd ..' do?
quiz.explanation: cd .. navigates to the parent directory.

quiz.question: Which flag shows hidden files with ls?
quiz.explanation: The -a flag shows all files including hidden ones starting with a dot.

exercises: Navigate to your home directory and list all hidden files.
Use pwd and cd to explore your filesystem, then return home.

lessons.title: File & Directory Operations
lessons.content:
File & Directory Operations

Create, copy, move, and delete files and directories with confidence.

Essential Commands

mkdir projects          # Create directory
touch app.js            # Create empty file
cp file.txt backup/     # Copy file
mv old.txt new.txt      # Rename/move file
rm file.txt             # Remove file
rm -rf dir/             # Remove directory recursively
ln -s target link       # Create symbolic link

Flags Explained

  • -r recursive (for directories)
  • -f force (no prompts)
  • -i interactive (confirm before delete)
  • -v verbose (show what's happening)

quiz.question: How do you create nested directories in one command?
quiz.explanation: mkdir -p creates parent directories as needed.

quiz.question: What does rm -rf do?
quiz.explanation: rm -rf recursively and forcefully removes files and directories. Use with extreme caution.

exercises: Create a directory structure: ~/practice/2024/january/notes
Create a file, copy it, rename the copy, then delete the original.

lessons.title: Power Tools
lessons.content:
Power Tools

Unix power tools are the secret weapons of productive developers. They compose together like LEGO blocks.

grep

grep "error" log.txt
grep -i "error" log.txt
grep -r "TODO" src/
grep -E "error|warn" log.txt

find

find . -name "*.js"
find . -type f -mtime -1
find . -type f -size +1M
find . -name "*.log" -delete

sed

sed 's/foo/bar/g' file.txt
sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt
sed -n '10,20p' file.txt

awk

awk '{print $1}' file.txt
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd
awk 'NR>1 {sum+=$3} END {print sum}' data.csv

xargs

find . -name "*.js" | xargs rm
find . -name "*.js" | xargs -I{} cp {} backup/

jq

cat data.json | jq '.name'
cat data.json | jq '.users[] | .email'
cat data.json | jq '. | {name, age}'

quiz.question: Which tool is best for processing JSON?
quiz.explanation: jq is specifically designed for querying and transforming JSON.

quiz.question: What does xargs do?
options: Builds and executes commands from stdin
quiz.explanation: xargs reads items from stdin and executes commands with those items as arguments.

quiz.question: How do you replace all occurrences with sed?
quiz.explanation: The 'g' flag means global — replace all occurrences on each line.

exercises: Find all .txt files in your home directory and count them.
Use grep and awk to extract the second column from a CSV where the first column matches a pattern.
Use jq to extract all email addresses from a JSON array of users.

lessons.title: Shell Scripting & Automation
lessons.content:
Shell Scripting & Automation

Shell scripts let you automate repetitive tasks, create build pipelines, and orchestrate complex workflows.

Basic Script Structure

#!/bin/bash
# My first script
set -euo pipefail

echo "Hello, $USER!"
echo "Today is $(date +%Y-%m-%d)"

Variables & Arguments

NAME="World"
echo "Hello, $NAME"

SCRIPT_NAME=$0
FIRST_ARG=$1
ALL_ARGS=$@

Conditionals

if [ -f "file.txt" ]; then
  echo "File exists"
fi

if [ $COUNT -gt 10 ]; then
  echo "Count is greater than 10"
fi

Loops

for file in *.txt; do
  echo "Processing $file"
done

COUNTER=0
while [ $COUNTER -lt 5 ]; do
  echo "Counter: $COUNTER"
  ((COUNTER++))
done

Functions

greet() {
  local name=$1
  echo "Hello, $name!"
}

Cron Jobs

crontab -e
0 2 * * * /path/to/backup.sh
*/15 * * * * /path/to/check.sh

quiz.question: What does set -euo pipefail do?
quiz.explanation: It makes the script exit on errors, unset variables, and pipe failures.

quiz.question: How do you pass arguments to a script?
quiz.explanation: Arguments are accessed positionally: $1, $2, etc.

exercises: Write a script that backs up all .txt files to a backup directory with timestamps.
Create a script that checks if a website is up and logs the result.
Set up a cron job that runs your backup script every day at midnight.

lessons.title: Advanced Terminal
lessons.content:
Advanced Terminal

Level up your terminal with modern tools that boost productivity and remote management capabilities.

tmux

tmux new -s mysession
tmux attach -t mysession
Ctrl+B %  # Split vertically
Ctrl+B "  # Split horizontally
Ctrl+B c  # New window
Ctrl+B d  # Detach

fzf

fzf
cat file.txt | fzf
Ctrl+T    # Fuzzy file completion
Ctrl+R    # Fuzzy history search

zoxide

z docs
z foo bar
zi
zoxide add ~/projects

SSH

ssh user@server.com
ssh -i key.pem user@host
scp file.txt user@host:/path
rsync -avz local/ user@host:remote/

Aliases

alias ll='ls -lah'
alias gs='git status'
alias gp='git pull'
alias ..='cd ..'

quiz.question: What is tmux used for?
quiz.explanation: tmux lets you run multiple terminal sessions in one window.

quiz.question: What does zoxide do?
quiz.explanation: zoxide learns your navigation habits and lets you jump to directories with fuzzy matching.

exercises: Set up a tmux session with 3 panes for coding, testing, and logs.
Configure 5 useful aliases in your shell config.
Set up SSH key authentication to a remote server.

lessons.title: Git Fundamentals & Commits
lessons.content:
Git Fundamentals

Git is a distributed version control system. Every developer has a full copy of the repository history.

Core Commands

git init
git clone url
git status
git add file.txt
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git log --oneline
git diff

Good Commit Messages

  • Use present tense
  • Keep first line under 50 characters
  • Use conventional commits: feat:, fix:, docs:, refactor:

quiz.question: What does 'git add .' do?
options: Adds all files to the repo
Stages all changes in current directory
quiz.explanation: git add . stages all changes in the current directory and subdirectories.

exercises: Initialize a repo, make changes, and create 3 commits with good messages.

lessons.title: Branching, Merging & Remotes
lessons.content:
Branching & Merging

Branches let you work on features independently without affecting the main codebase.

Branch Commands

git branch feature-x
git checkout feature-x
git checkout -b feature-x
git branch -d feature-x
git merge feature-x

Remotes

git remote -v
git remote add origin url
git push -u origin main
git pull origin main
git fetch origin

quiz.question: What does 'git checkout -b' do?
quiz.explanation: git checkout -b creates a new branch and immediately switches to it.

exercises: Create a feature branch, make changes, merge it back to main, then delete it.

lessons.title: Rebasing, Cherry-pick & Bisect
lessons.content:
Advanced Git Operations

Rebasing

git rebase main
git rebase -i HEAD~3

Cherry-pick

git cherry-pick abc123
git cherry-pick abc123..def456

Bisect

git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD
git bisect good v1.0
git bisect run ./test.sh

quiz.question: When should you use rebase over merge?
options: When you want a linear history
When you want to preserve all branches
quiz.explanation: Rebase creates a cleaner, linear history by replaying commits.

exercises: Do an interactive rebase to squash 3 commits into one.
Use git bisect to find which commit broke a feature.

lessons.title: Submodules & GitHub Actions
lessons.content:
Submodules & CI/CD

Git Submodules

git submodule add url path
git submodule update --init
git clone --recursive url

GitHub Actions Basics

name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build

quiz.question: What file extension do GitHub Actions workflows use?
quiz.explanation: GitHub Actions workflows are YAML files in .github/workflows/.

exercises: Add a submodule to an existing project and clone it elsewhere.
Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs tests on every push.

lessons.title: Professional Git Workflows
lessons.content:
Professional Workflows

Git Flow

main        # Production-ready code
develop     # Integration branch
feature/*   # Feature branches
release/*   # Release preparation
hotfix/*    # Production fixes

Trunk-Based Development

  • Short-lived feature branches (under 24 hours)
  • Feature flags for incomplete work
  • Continuous integration

PR Best Practices

  • Keep PRs small and focused
  • Write clear descriptions
  • Add tests
  • Request review from relevant team members

quiz.question: What is trunk-based development?
options: Using only main branch with short-lived features
quiz.explanation: Trunk-based development keeps code in main with feature flags for unfinished work.

exercises: Simulate a Git Flow workflow with feature, release, and hotfix branches.
Create a PR template for your project.

lessons.title: Hono Routing & Middleware
lessons.content:
Hono Fundamentals

Hono is a lightweight, fast web framework for building APIs. It works on Node.js, Deno, Bun, and edge runtimes.

Basic Server

import { Hono } from 'hono'
const app = new Hono()

app.get('/', (c) => c.text('Hello Hono!'))
app.get('/json', (c) => c.json({ message: 'ok' }))

export default app

Routing

app.get('/users', listUsers)
app.post('/users', createUser)
app.get('/users/:id', getUser)
app.put('/users/:id', updateUser)
app.delete('/users/:id', deleteUser)

Middleware

app.use('*', logger())
app.use('*', cors())
app.use('/admin/*', authMiddleware)

app.use(async (c, next) => {
  const start = Date.now()
  await next()
  const duration = Date.now() - start
  console.log(`${c.req.method} ${c.req.url} - ${duration}ms`)
})

quiz.question: Which method creates a route parameter?
quiz.explanation: :id is a route parameter that captures the URL segment.

exercises: Create a Hono server with GET, POST, PUT, DELETE routes for a resource.

lessons.title: Validation & Error Handling
lessons.content:
Validation & Error Handling

Zod Validation

import { z } from 'zod'
import { zValidator } from '@hono/zod-validator'

const schema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1),
  email: z.string().email(),
  age: z.number().min(0).max(150)
})

app.post('/users', zValidator('json', schema), (c) => {
  const data = c.req.valid('json')
  return c.json({ success: true, data })
})

Error Handling

app.onError((err, c) => {
  console.error(`${err}`)
  return c.json({ error: err.message, status: 500 }, 500)
})

quiz.question: What does Zod do?
quiz.explanation: Zod is a TypeScript-first schema validation library.

exercises: Create a Hono endpoint with Zod validation for user registration.

lessons.title: CORS, Rate Limiting & File Uploads
lessons.content:
Advanced Hono Features

CORS

import { cors } from 'hono/cors'

app.use(cors({
  origin: ['http://localhost:5173', 'https://myapp.com'],
  allowMethods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],
  credentials: true
}))

Rate Limiting

import { rateLimiter } from 'hono-rate-limiter'

app.use(rateLimiter({
  windowMs: 60 * 1000,
  max: 100,
  keyGenerator: (c) => c.req.header('x-forwarded-for') || 'anonymous'
}))

File Uploads

import { writeFile } from 'node:fs/promises'

app.post('/upload', async (c) => {
  const body = await c.req.parseBody()
  const file = body.file as File
  const arrayBuffer = await file.arrayBuffer()
  await writeFile(`./uploads/${file.name}`, Buffer.from(arrayBuffer))
  return c.json({ uploaded: file.name })
})

quiz.question: What does CORS stand for?
quiz.explanation: CORS allows servers to specify who can access their resources from different origins.

exercises: Set up CORS for multiple allowed origins.
Implement rate limiting on an API endpoint.
Create a file upload endpoint with size validation.

lessons.title: Database Integration & Testing
lessons.content:
Database Integration

Drizzle with Hono

import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql2'
import { createConnection } from 'mysql2/promise'
import { Hono } from 'hono'

const connection = createConnection(process.env.DATABASE_URL!)
const db = drizzle(await connection, { mode: 'planetscale', schema })

const app = new Hono()

app.get('/users', async (c) => {
  const users = await db.query.users.findMany()
  return c.json(users)
})

Testing with Vitest

import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest'
import app from './app'

describe('API', () => {
  it('GET / returns hello', async () => {
    const res = await app.request('/')
    expect(res.status).toBe(200)
    expect(await res.text()).toBe('Hello Hono!')
  })
})

quiz.question: Which testing framework is recommended with Hono?
quiz.explanation: Vitest is fast, modern, and works great with Vite-based projects including Hono.

exercises: Connect Drizzle ORM to a Hono app and create CRUD endpoints.
Write tests for your Hono API endpoints.

lessons.title: Production Patterns & Security
lessons.content:
Production Patterns

Environment Variables

import { env } from './lib/env'

const app = new Hono()
app.use('*', async (c, next) => {
  c.set('db', db)
  await next()
})

Security Headers

import { secureHeaders } from 'hono/secure-headers'
app.use(secureHeaders())

Request Logging

app.use('*', logger())
app.use('*', async (c, next) => {
  console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${c.req.method} ${c.req.path}`)
  await next()
})

Graceful Shutdown

process.on('SIGTERM', () => {
  console.log('SIGTERM received, shutting down gracefully')
  server.close(() => {
    console.log('Server closed')
    process.exit(0)
  })
})

quiz.question: What does secureHeaders middleware do?
quiz.explanation: It automatically sets security headers like X-Content-Type-Options and X-Frame-Options.

exercises: Add security headers to your Hono app.
Implement graceful shutdown handling.
Set up structured request logging.

lessons.title: Schema Design & Types
lessons.content:
Schema Design with Drizzle

Drizzle ORM provides type-safe SQL-like syntax for defining database schemas.

Basic Table

import { mysqlTable, serial, varchar, text, timestamp } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql-core'

export const users = mysqlTable('users', {
  id: serial('id').primaryKey(),
  name: varchar('name', { length: 255 }).notNull(),
  email: varchar('email', { length: 320 }).notNull().unique(),
  bio: text('bio'),
  createdAt: timestamp('createdAt').defaultNow().notNull()
})

Column Types

serial('id')                    // Auto-increment primary key
varchar('name', { length: 255 }) // Variable string
text('content')                 // Long text
int('age')                      // Integer
boolean('active')               // Boolean
bigint('userId', { mode: 'number', unsigned: true }) // Foreign key
timestamp('createdAt').defaultNow() // Timestamp

quiz.question: What does serial() create?
quiz.explanation: serial() creates a bigint unsigned auto_increment primary key.

exercises: Design a schema for a blog with users, posts, and comments.

lessons.title: CRUD Operations
lessons.content:
CRUD with Drizzle

Create

const [{ id }] = await db.insert(users).values({
  name: 'John Doe',
  email: 'john@example.com'
}).$returningId()

Read

const allUsers = await db.select().from(users)

const user = await db.select().from(users)
  .where(eq(users.id, 1))
  .limit(1)

const user = await db.query.users.findFirst({
  where: eq(users.id, 1)
})

Update & Delete

await db.update(users)
  .set({ name: 'Jane Doe' })
  .where(eq(users.id, 1))

await db.delete(users)
  .where(eq(users.id, 1))

quiz.question: How do you get the inserted ID in MySQL?
quiz.explanation: MySQL doesn't support RETURNING, so Drizzle provides $returningId() specifically for getting the auto-increment ID.

exercises: Implement full CRUD operations for a users table using Drizzle.

lessons.title: Relations & Joins
lessons.content:
Relations & Joins

Defining Relations

import { relations } from 'drizzle-orm'

export const usersRelations = relations(users, ({ many }) => ({
  posts: many(posts)
}))

export const postsRelations = relations(posts, ({ one, many }) => ({
  author: one(users, { fields: [posts.authorId], references: [users.id] }),
  comments: many(comments)
}))

Querying with Relations

const usersWithPosts = await db.query.users.findMany({
  with: { posts: true }
})

const postsWithAuthor = await db.query.posts.findMany({
  with: { author: true, comments: true }
})

quiz.question: What mode is needed for TiDB compatibility?
quiz.explanation: mode: 'planetscale' avoids LATERAL JOINs which TiDB doesn't support.

exercises: Set up one-to-many and many-to-many relations in Drizzle.

lessons.title: Migrations & Transactions
lessons.content:
Migrations & Transactions

Migrations

# Generate migration
npm run db:generate

# Apply migration
npm run db:migrate

# Push schema directly (dev)
npm run db:push

Transactions

await db.transaction(async (tx) => {
  const [{ id }] = await tx.insert(users).values({ name: 'Alice' }).$returningId()
  await tx.insert(posts).values({
    title: 'Hello',
    authorId: id
  })
})

quiz.question: What happens if a transaction fails halfway?
options: Only the last change rolls back
quiz.explanation: Transactions are atomic — all changes succeed or all roll back together.

exercises: Create a transaction that inserts a user and their first post atomically.

lessons.title: Indexing & Query Optimization
lessons.content:
Indexing & Optimization

Indexes

import { index } from 'drizzle-orm/mysql-core'

export const posts = mysqlTable('posts', {
  id: serial('id').primaryKey(),
  authorId: bigint('authorId', { mode: 'number', unsigned: true }).notNull(),
  title: varchar('title', { length: 255 }).notNull(),
  slug: varchar('slug', { length: 255 }).notNull().unique(),
  createdAt: timestamp('createdAt').defaultNow().notNull()
}, (table) => ({
  authorIdx: index('author_idx').on(table.authorId),
  createdIdx: index('created_idx').on(table.createdAt)
}))

Query Tips

  • Select only columns you need
  • Use indexes for frequent WHERE columns
  • Use EXPLAIN to analyze queries
  • Avoid N+1 queries with joins/relations
demoSteps: > EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM posts WHERE authorId = 1

quiz.question: When should you create an index?
options: On columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY
quiz.explanation: Indexes speed up queries but slow down writes. Use them strategically on frequently queried columns.

exercises: Add indexes to optimize a slow query in your application.

lessons.title: VPS Setup with Ubuntu
lessons.content:
VPS Setup

Setting up a production server from scratch gives you full control over your environment.

Initial Server Setup

sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

adduser deployer
usermod -aG sudo deployer

sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sudo systemctl restart sshd

sudo ufw allow OpenSSH
sudo ufw allow 'Nginx Full'
sudo ufw enable

quiz.question: Why disable root SSH login?
quiz.explanation: Disabling root login prevents brute force attacks on the most targeted account.

exercises: Set up a new Ubuntu server with a non-root user, SSH keys, and firewall.

lessons.title: Nginx & SSL with Certbot
lessons.content:
Nginx & SSL

Install Nginx

sudo apt install nginx
sudo systemctl start nginx
sudo systemctl enable nginx

Server Block

server {
  listen 80;
  server_name example.com;
  location / {
    proxy_pass http://localhost:3000;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection 'upgrade';
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_cache_bypass $http_upgrade;
  }
}

SSL with Certbot

sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx
sudo certbot --nginx -d example.com
sudo certbot renew --dry-run

quiz.question: What does Certbot do?
quiz.explanation: Certbot automates obtaining and renewing free SSL certificates from Let's Encrypt.

exercises: Configure Nginx as a reverse proxy and add SSL with Certbot.

lessons.title: PM2 Process Management
lessons.content:
PM2 Process Management

PM2 is a production process manager for Node.js applications.

Basic Commands

npm install -g pm2
pm2 start app.js --name api
pm2 restart api
pm2 stop api
pm2 delete api
pm2 list
pm2 logs api
pm2 monit

PM2 Ecosystem Config

module.exports = {
  apps: [{
    name: 'api',
    script: './dist/boot.js',
    instances: 'max',
    exec_mode: 'cluster',
    env: { NODE_ENV: 'production' },
    log_date_format: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z'
  }]
}

quiz.question: What does PM2 do?
quiz.explanation: PM2 manages Node.js processes with clustering, logs, monitoring, and auto-restart.

exercises: Set up PM2 with an ecosystem config for your application.

lessons.title: Docker Basics
lessons.content:
Docker Basics

Docker containers package your application with all its dependencies.

Core Concepts

docker pull node:20
docker run -it node:20 bash
docker ps
docker ps -a
docker stop container_id
docker rm container_id

Dockerfile

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

Docker Compose

version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
  db:
    image: mysql:8
    environment:
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: secret
demoSteps: Sending build context to Docker daemon

quiz.question: What is a Dockerfile?
options: A config file for Docker containers
quiz.explanation: A Dockerfile contains instructions to build a Docker image.

exercises: Create a Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml for your application.

lessons.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
lessons.content:
CI/CD Pipelines

Continuous Integration and Deployment automate testing and deployment.

Full Pipeline

name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run test
      - run: npm run check
  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to VPS
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@master
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.HOST }}
          username: deployer
          key: ${{ secrets.SSH_KEY }}
          script: |
            cd /var/www/app
            git pull origin main
            npm ci
            npm run build
            pm2 restart api

quiz.question: What does CI/CD stand for?
quiz.explanation: CI/CD automates the process of integrating code changes and deploying them to production.

exercises: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that tests and deploys your app on push to main.

lessons.title: OAuth 2.0 Flows
lessons.content:
OAuth 2.0

OAuth 2.0 is the industry standard for authorization. It lets users grant limited access to their resources without sharing passwords.

The Flow

  1. User clicks "Sign in with Google"
  2. App redirects to Google authorization server
  3. User authenticates and approves scopes
  4. Google redirects back with authorization code
  5. App exchanges code for access token
  6. App uses token to fetch user profile

Authorization URL

const url = new URL('https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth')
url.searchParams.set('client_id', CLIENT_ID)
url.searchParams.set('redirect_uri', REDIRECT_URI)
url.searchParams.set('response_type', 'code')
url.searchParams.set('scope', 'openid email profile')
url.searchParams.set('state', csrfToken)

quiz.question: What does the authorization code do?
quiz.explanation: The authorization code is a temporary credential exchanged for access tokens.

exercises: Implement an OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow from scratch.

lessons.title: JWT Sessions & Cookies
lessons.content:
JWT Sessions

JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are compact, self-contained tokens for transmitting information between parties.

Token Structure

{ "alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT" }

{ "sub": "123", "name": "Dev", "iat": 1704067200 }

HMACSHA256(base64(header) + "." + base64(payload), secret)

Session Cookie

import { serialize } from 'cookie'

const token = await signJWT({ userId: user.id })
const cookie = serialize('session', token, {
  httpOnly: true,
  secure: true,
  sameSite: 'lax',
  maxAge: 60 * 60 * 24 * 7
})

quiz.question: What does httpOnly prevent?
quiz.explanation: httpOnly cookies cannot be accessed by JavaScript, preventing XSS token theft.

exercises: Implement JWT sign and verify functions with proper cookie settings.

lessons.title: Protected Routes & Guards
lessons.content:
Protected Routes

Implement authentication guards to protect sensitive endpoints and pages.

tRPC Middleware

const requireAuth = t.middleware(async (opts) => {
  const { ctx, next } = opts
  if (!ctx.user) {
    throw new TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED' })
  }
  return next({ ctx: { ...ctx, user: ctx.user } })
})

export const authedQuery = t.procedure.use(requireAuth)

React Route Guard

function ProtectedRoute({ children }) {
  const { user, isLoading } = useAuth({
    redirectOnUnauthenticated: true
  })
  if (isLoading) return <LoadingSpinner />
  if (!user) return null
  return children
}

quiz.question: Where should authentication checks happen?
quiz.explanation: Always validate on the backend — frontend checks are for UX only and can be bypassed.

exercises: Create an authenticated tRPC procedure and a React route guard.

lessons.title: Refresh Tokens & Security
lessons.content:
Refresh Tokens

Access tokens expire quickly. Refresh tokens let users stay logged in without re-authenticating frequently.

Token Strategy

Access Token:  Short-lived (15 minutes)
Refresh Token: Long-lived (7 days), stored in httpOnly cookie

app.post('/refresh', async (c) => {
  const refreshToken = getCookie(c, 'refresh_token')
  const payload = await verifyRefreshToken(refreshToken)
  const newAccessToken = await signAccessToken({ userId: payload.userId })
  return c.json({ accessToken: newAccessToken })
})

Security Best Practices

  • Rotate refresh tokens on use
  • Store tokens in httpOnly cookies
  • Bind tokens to device/session
  • Implement token revocation
demoSteps: 3. Server verifies and issues new access token

quiz.question: Why use refresh tokens?
options: They reduce risk of long-lived access tokens
quiz.explanation: Short-lived access tokens limit exposure if stolen. Refresh tokens can be revoked and rotated.

exercises: Implement a refresh token flow with rotation and revocation.

lessons.title: Role-Based Access Control
lessons.content:
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

RBAC restricts system access based on user roles.

Implementation

function requireRole(role: string) {
  return t.middleware(async (opts) => {
    const { ctx, next } = opts
    if (!ctx.user || ctx.user.role !== role) {
      throw new TRPCError({ code: 'FORBIDDEN' })
    }
    return next({ ctx })
  })
}

export const adminQuery = authedQuery.use(requireRole('admin'))

role: mysqlEnum('role', ['student', 'superadmin']).default('student')

UI Guards

function AdminOnly({ children }) {
  const { user } = useAuth()
  if (user?.role !== 'superadmin') return <Forbidden />
  return children
}

quiz.question: What does RBAC stand for?
quiz.explanation: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individual users.

exercises: Implement admin and user roles with middleware guards and UI conditionals.
